request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"38-studios::gaming","name":"38 Studios","one_liner":"American AAA game studio that built a $150M MMORPG franchise (Project Copernicus / Kingdoms of Amalur) on government-backed debt, collapsed in 2012 when sales fell short of break-even and funding dried up.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":2,"rationale":"38 Studios relied on one-time purchase (boxed/digital retail) sales of a single-player RPG plus a planned subscription-based MMORPG — the opposite of the new pitch's free-to-play model monetized through cosmetics and a battle pass. The revenue architecture, risk profile, and player acquisition funnel are fundamentally different."},"market":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both are in the consumer gaming market targeting a broad audience. However, 38 Studios aimed at premium PC/console RPG and MMORPG audiences, while the new pitch targets the mobile-first (iOS/Android) and Steam free-to-play 4X strategy segment — a different genre and platform mix, though the overlap in gaming sector justifies a mid score."},"gtm":{"score":3,"rationale":"38 Studios relied on a major publisher (EA) for distribution and marketing of its boxed retail title, with no organic free-to-play funnel. The new pitch's F2P-on-mobile approach uses app store distribution and viral/organic growth — a very different go-to-market motion, though both seek wide consumer reach."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"38 Studios was a well-funded, large (400-person) studio with $150M in total spending; the new pitch appears to be an early-stage indie/startup. Both were pre-revenue at the launch of their flagship product, but 38 Studios' scale, burn rate, and debt structure dwarf what a typical F2P indie startup would carry."}},"why_similar":"Both are consumer-facing gaming companies building original IP in a competitive market, with ambitions to build a multi-game franchise. Both face the challenge of sustaining development burn before achieving sufficient player monetization. The gaming sector, customer type (consumer), and the fundamental risk of over-investing in a product before validating market fit are shared.","where_diverged":"1. **Monetization model**: 38 Studios used one-time purchase (boxed retail/digital) per the Trusted facts; the new pitch is free-to-play with cosmetics and battle pass — a recurring, lower-friction revenue model. 2. **Platform**: 38 Studios targeted PC/console; the new pitch targets iOS, Android, and Steam simultaneously, including mobile-first. 3. **Genre**: 38 Studios built an MMORPG and single-player RPG; the new pitch is a 4X strategy title. 4. **Scale and funding**: 38 Studios employed ~400 people and burned $150M+ of government loan money; the new pitch is presumably a lean startup. 5. **Publisher dependency**: 38 Studios depended on EA for publishing; the new pitch is self-publishing via app stores and Steam.","failure_causes":["Over-reliance on a single government loan ($75M) with no viable fallback funding","Launch title (Kingdoms of Amalur) missed break-even sales threshold (1.3M vs. 2M needed)","Runway entirely consumed before flagship MMO (Project Copernicus) could ship","Founder lacked business experience leading to executive dysfunction and mismanagement","Venture capital market declined to invest due to perceived market risk of MMO genre","Political interference and hostile public statements accelerated creditor default","Cost structure ($150M+ in total debt) wildly incommensurate with revenue actually generated"],"lessons_for_input":["Validate that your monetization unit economics (ARPU, LTV, CAC) work before scaling headcount or budget — 38 Studios burned $150M without confirming players would pay enough to break even.","Avoid single-point-of-failure financing; diversify across multiple small investors or revenue milestones rather than depending on one large government or institutional loan.","Ship a minimum viable product on your target platform early to test the free-to-play funnel and battle pass conversion rate before building out the full 4X feature set.","Keep your burn rate proportional to validated revenue: 38 Studios' 400-person studio was built on projected profits that never materialized; a lean team extends runway and preserves optionality.","Plan for the genre's competitive dynamics — 38 Studios underestimated how crowded the MMORPG market was; similarly, free-to-play 4X on mobile is highly competitive, so identify a clear differentiator early."]}
